behaviors.description: The clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract
behaviors.justification: Key term identification is fundamentally a capability of the language model. A pure mock of LLM output would hide failures where the real model fails to recognize key entities in actual contract documents. The model's ability to extract these terms depends on its learned representations, not on surrounding code.
behaviors.test_description: Run the clause extractor on real contracts containing key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) using the real language model. Verify the extractor identifies all required entity types. Test with contracts where terms appear in standard locations and where they are embedded in complex clauses to catch failures in entity recognition depth.

behaviors.description: The risk assessor flags language that would be problematic under the configured risk policy, even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic
behaviors.justification: Detecting indirect or euphemistic language requires semantic understanding that only the real model can provide. Mocking risk assessments would completely hide failures where the model misses subtle or obfuscated problematic language. The boundary between flagged and unflagged language depends on what the actual model learns about risk signals.
behaviors.test_description: Run the risk assessor on contracts containing problematic clauses expressed indirectly (e.g., 'may adjust compensation' instead of 'can reduce payment') and verify the real model flags these even when phrased euphemistically. Include test cases with varying risk policies to confirm the model respects policy configuration.

behaviors.description: The compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction
behaviors.justification: Regulatory compliance assessment depends on the model's knowledge of actual laws and regulations for specific jurisdictions. Mocking compliance results would hide failures where the model misunderstands requirements or fails to recognize jurisdiction-specific constraints. This is intrinsically a model behavior, not a code logic issue.
behaviors.test_description: Run the compliance checker on contracts with known compliance gaps for a specific jurisdiction (e.g., missing required regulatory disclosures for EU contracts). Verify the real model correctly identifies these gaps. Test with multiple jurisdictions to ensure the checker responds appropriately to jurisdiction configuration changes.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments (e.g., extractor says 'standard' but risk assessor says 'high risk'), the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution
behaviors.justification: The merge function is deterministic logic that could be tested in isolation with mocked inputs. However, mocks risk missing format incompatibilities: if mock outputs don't exactly match the real reviewer output schema, the test would pass while production merge logic breaks on real outputs. MOCK-MISS occurs when mock format diverges from actual reviewer JSON/structure. Integration testing with actual (or precisely-specified) reviewer outputs catches format-specific failures.
behaviors.test_description: Wire all three reviewers together on the same contract and deliberately create a scenario where two reviewers flag the same clause differently (e.g., extractor identifies a clause as 'standard obligation', risk assessor flags it as 'high risk'). Verify the merged report contains both assessments, clearly attributed to each reviewer with no data loss or conflation.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report — completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires orchestrating multiple agentic steps in sequence: (1) dispatch to three parallel reviewers (each depends on real LLM non-determinism), (2) collect their results, (3) invoke merge on those results. A failure in any step or in the coordination between steps would be missed by lower levels. Unit/Integration testing of individual components would miss orchestration failures (e.g., missing result collection, improper parallelism, merge invoked before collection completes).
behaviors.test_description: Run the complete pipeline on a real contract: dispatch the contract to all three reviewers in parallel, collect their outputs in real-time, invoke the merge step on collected results, and verify the final unified report contains all findings from all three reviewers without omission. Include assertions that the pipeline respects parallelism (reviewers run concurrently) and that the merge receives outputs from all three.
